mechanism_narrative: An increase in total nonfarm payrolls signals expanding aggregate income and consumption demand, which drives corporate revenue growth and produces positive S&P 500 returns over the following 20 trading days.
failure_condition: The hypothesis is rejected if walk-forward regressions show PAYEMS has a negative sign or t-statistic magnitude below 1.5 when predicting 20-day SPX returns, or if average returns in top PAYEMS quintiles are not higher than bottom quintiles.

mechanism_narrative: A federal funds rate sustained above 4.5% reflects restrictive monetary policy that elevates discount rates and corporate borrowing costs, compressing equity valuations and generating negative S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: The hypothesis is rejected if S&P 500 20-day forward returns are positive in more than 50% of months when FEDFUNDS exceeds 4.5%, or if the mean return during triggered periods is not significantly negative.

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneous month-over-month growth in retail sales and a decline in the unemployment rate jointly confirm a broad-based demand expansion backed by labor market strength; this combination predicts positive S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days.
candidate_trigger: RSAFS increases month-over-month and UNRATE declines month-over-month
failure_condition: The hypothesis is rejected if the joint occurrence of rising RSAFS and falling UNRATE fails to produce positive average 60-day SPX returns, or if a regression interaction term between the two series has a negative or statistically insignificant coefficient.

mechanism_narrative: Rising housing starts predict positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days specifically when unemployment is elevated above 4.5%, because in this regime housing signals policy transmission and imminent labor absorption; the signal is absent when unemployment is low.
failure_condition: The hypothesis is rejected if positive HOUST changes during UNRATE > 4.5% do not yield significantly positive average 20-day SPX returns, or if the signal performs equally well when UNRATE is below 4.5%.

mechanism_narrative: Month-over-month growth in industrial production indicates expanding real output and capacity utilization, which supports near-term earnings expectations and produces positive S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days.
failure_condition: The hypothesis is rejected if positive monthly changes in INDPRO are followed by negative average 5-day SPX returns, or if the rank correlation between INDPRO changes and 5-day forward returns is negative over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A cumulative increase in the unemployment rate exceeding 0.3 percentage points over three months signals deteriorating labor income and aggregate demand, which reduces corporate earnings expectations and generates negative S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE rises more than 0.3 percentage points over 3 months
failure_condition: The hypothesis is rejected if SPX 20-day returns are positive in more than half of the instances where UNRATE rises by more than 0.3 percentage points over three months.

mechanism_narrative: Two consecutive monthly declines in industrial production indicate a contraction in real economic output, which erodes near-term earnings forecasts and produces negative S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days.
failure_condition: The hypothesis is rejected if consecutive monthly INDPRO declines are followed by positive average 5-day SPX returns in more than 50% of occurrences.

mechanism_narrative: Quarterly real GDP growth signals the economy's aggregate demand trajectory; positive momentum in GDPC1 drives corporate earnings expansion and predicts positive S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days.
failure_condition: The hypothesis is rejected if positive quarterly changes in GDPC1 do not produce significantly positive average 60-day SPX returns, or if the rank correlation between GDPC1 growth and 60-day forward returns is below 0.03 over any 5-year window.
